source_documents.name: NuScale FSAR Ch.01
source_documents.description: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Chapter 01 - Introduction and General Description

source_documents.name: NuScale FSAR Ch.05
source_documents.description: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Chapter 05 - Reactor Coolant System

questions.question: What is the thermal output of the NuScale Power Module (NPM)?
questions.expected_answer: Each NPM has a thermal output of 160 MWt, resulting in a total of 1,920 MWt based on 12 modules.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Each NPM is rated at 160 MWt (1,920 MWt, total), with approximately 50 MWe (600 MWe total) output

questions.question: How many NPMs can be operated in a single Reactor Building at the NuScale power plant?
questions.expected_answer: A single Reactor Building can operate from 1 to a maximum of 12 Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: from one (1) to twelve (12) NPMs operate within a single Reactor Building

questions.question: In the NuScale design, is AC or DC power required for safety shutdown and cooling?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale design does not require AC or DC power for safety shutdown and cooling.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: No AC or DC power required for safe shutdown and cooling

questions.question: What is the design configuration of NuScale's steam generator?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale steam generator is designed as a small helical coil located outside the reactor pressure boundary.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the outside of the tubes

questions.question: What is the internal pressure condition of the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) during normal operation?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale containment vessel maintains a sub-atmospheric condition during normal operation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Sub-atmospheric containment pressure during normal operation

questions.question: What ASME code classification is the NuScale containment vessel (CNV) designed to?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale CNV is designed as an ASME BPVC Class MC (steel) containment vessel and will be constructed as an ASME BPVC Class 1 pressure vessel.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The CNV is an ASME BPVC Class MC (steel) containment that is designed, analyzed, fabricated, inspected, tested and stamped as an ASME BPVC Class 1 pressure vessel

questions.question: How is the reactor coolant circulation conducted in the NuScale NPM?
questions.expected_answer: NuScale circulates the reactor coolant through natural circulation and does not utilize a reactor coolant pump.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Natural circulation provides reactor coolant system flow, thereby eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps

questions.question: What is the electrical output of a NuScale NPM per module?
questions.expected_answer: Each NPM produces approximately 50 MWe of electrical output, resulting in a total of 600 MWe based on 12 modules.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Each NPM is rated at 160 MWt (1,920 MWt, total), with approximately 50 MWe (600 MWe total) output

questions.question: What is the net electrical output considering the self-consumption of the 12 NPM facilities?
questions.expected_answer: The total net output, considering the house loads of the 12 NPM facilities, is approximately 570 MWe.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: When considering house loads, the total net output is approximately 570 MWe for a 12 NPM facility

questions.question: What type of reactor is the NuScale NSSS?
questions.expected_answer: The NuScale NSSS is a passive small modular pressurized water reactor.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The NuScale NSSS is a passive NuScale-designed small modular pressurized water reactor

questions.question: What are the main functions of the RCS (Reactor Coolant System)?
questions.expected_answer: The RCS circulates the primary coolant to transfer approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the core to the steam generator system.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RCS promotes natural circulation to transfer approximately 160 MW thermal power from the reactor core to the steam generator system

questions.question: What are the main components included in the Reactor Coolant System (RCS)?
questions.expected_answer: The RCS includes the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), the integrated pressurizer (PZR), the internal structures of the reactor vessel (RVI), the reactor safety valves (RSV), the RCS piping within the containment (CNV), and instrumentation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RCS includes the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and integral pressurizer (PZR), the reactor vessel internals (RVI), the reactor safety valves (RSVs)

questions.question: What is the motive force for natural circulation in NuScale?
questions.expected_answer: The driving force for natural circulation is the density difference between the high-temperature coolant exiting the reactor core and the low-temperature coolant from the steam generator, as well as the height difference between the reactor core (heat source) and the steam generator (heat sink).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The motive force for the reactor coolant flow is natural convection, driven by differences in coolant density between the hot coolant leaving the reactor core and the colder coolant leaving the SGs, and by the elevation difference

questions.question: What is the configuration of the ECCS valves?
questions.expected_answer: The ECCS valves consist of three RVVs (Reactor Vent Valves) and two RRVs (Reactor Recirculation Valves).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The ECCS valves consist of three RVVs and two RRVs

questions.question: How many reactor safety valves (RSVs) are there, and where are they connected?
questions.expected_answer: Two pilot-operated self-actuating relief valves (RSVs) are connected to the top of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head and discharge directly to the containment vessel (CNV).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Two pilot operated, self-actuating RSVs connect to the top of the RPV upper head and discharge directly to the CNV

questions.question: Describe the design characteristics of the steam generator (SG).
questions.expected_answer: The steam generator (SG) features a once-through helical coil design, where the primary side reactor coolant flows outside the tubes, while the secondary fluid flows inside the tubes.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The SGs are a once-through, helical-coil design, with primary-side reactor coolant outside the tubes and secondary-side fluid inside the tubes

questions.question: How many independent steam generators (SGs) are there in NuScale?
questions.expected_answer: Two independent yet intertwined steam generators (SG) are located inside the reactor pressure vessel (RPV).

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The design contains two independent, but intertwined, SGs located inside the RPV

questions.question: Where is the Pressurizer (PZR) located in relation to the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV)?
questions.expected_answer: The pressurizer is integrated with the RPV and occupies the internal volume of the RPV above the PZR baffle plate.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The PZR, which is integral to the RPV, occupies the volume inside the RPV above the PZR baffle plate

questions.question: What are the main functions of the pressurizer?
questions.expected_answer: The pressurizer maintains a saturated condition of liquid and steam in equilibrium at a temperature higher than Thot for pressure control of the RCS, and it also serves as a surge volume.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The PZR provides a point in the RCS where liquid and vapor are maintained in equilibrium under saturated conditions at a temperature greater than Thot for pressure control

questions.question: What lines make up the RCS piping outside the RPV?
questions.expected_answer: The system consists of two PZR spray lines branched from a common spray header, along with the RPV high-point vent line, RCS injection line, and RCS discharge line.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: two PZR spray line branches from a common spray header, RPV high point degasification line, RCS injection line, RCS discharge line

questions.question: What are the main subassemblies of the RVI (Reactor Vessel Internals)?
questions.expected_answer: The components include the core support assembly, surveillance specimen assembly, lower riser assembly, upper riser assembly, flow distributor, and PZR spray nozzle, among others.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RVI sub-assemblies include the core support assembly, surveillance capsule assemblies, lower riser assembly, upper riser assembly, flow diverter, and PZR spray nozzles

questions.question: Where is the Reactor Vent Valve (RVV) connected, and what functions does it serve?
questions.expected_answer: The RVV is connected to the upper head of the RPV and directly releases steam from the PZR to the CNV, also providing overpressure protection during low-temperature operation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RVVs connect to the upper head portion of the RPV and discharge the PZR steam space directly to the CNV. The RVVs also provide overpressure protection during operations at low temperature conditions

questions.question: What is the location and function of the RRV (Reactor Recirculation Valve)?
questions.expected_answer: The two RRV (Reactor Relief Valves) are connected to the RPV (Reactor Pressure Vessel) shell directly above the main closure flange, and when opened, they recirculate water from the CNV (Containment Vessel) back into the RPV, allowing it to pass through the core.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Two RRVs connect to the RPV shell just above the main closure flange. When opened, they permit recirculation of water in the CNV back into the RPV and ultimately through the core

questions.question: What is the role of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS)?
questions.expected_answer: The DHRS is connected to the steam generator (SG) and removes decay heat from the primary coolant, while being connected to the steam and feedwater piping.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The DHRS is connected to the steam and feed piping to permit use of the SG to remove decay heat from the primary coolant

questions.question: What are the main features of the NuScale multi-unit power plant?
questions.expected_answer: The design allows for incremental increases in power capacity, featuring a scalable design, small modular nuclear reactors, and the capability for island mode operation.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: a scalable plant design, which allows for incremental plant capacity growth, a compact nuclear island, the ability to operate in island mode

questions.question: What are the components of the NuScale Power Module (NPM)?
questions.expected_answer: The NPM integrates the reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and is housed within a small steel containment structure.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The NPM is composed of a reactor core, a pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel

questions.question: What are the design advantages of having a vacuum inside the CNV during normal operation?
questions.expected_answer: The internal pressure of the CNV is maintained at a vacuum, eliminating the need for insulation between the reactor vessel and the CNV.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The CNV internal pressure is maintained at a vacuum during normal operations and as such insulation materials are not required between the reactor vessel and the CNV

questions.question: How is measurement uncertainty considered in the design output?
questions.expected_answer: The design output assumes an additional 2% to account for measurement uncertainty.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Design power assumes an additional 2 percent to account for measurement uncertainty

questions.question: Where is the containment vessel (CNV) installed?
questions.expected_answer: The containment vessel is installed on the partition wall of the Reactor Building module, with its lower section located within the Reactor Building pool.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The containment vessels are mounted to the Reactor Building module compartment walls and at the bottom within the Reactor Building pool

questions.question: What functions does the RPV perform, and what components does it support?
questions.expected_answer: The RPV serves as a barrier to the release of fission products as part of the RCPB, encompassing and supporting the core, RVI, steam generator, and pressurizer.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: The RPV is a metal vessel that forms part of the RCPB and is a barrier to the release of fission products. The RPV contains and supports the reactor core, RVI, steam generators, and PZR

questions.question: What is the nominal volume of the Hot Leg?
questions.expected_answer: The nominal volume of the Hot Leg (lower riser, riser transition, upper riser, riser supports) is 635 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Volumes
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Hot Leg (lower riser, riser transition, upper riser, riser supports): 635 ft³

questions.question: What is the nominal volume of the Cold Leg?
questions.expected_answer: The nominal volume of the Cold Leg (feedwater plenums, downcomer transition, downcomer, core barrel, RPV bottom head, flow diverter) is 578 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Volumes
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Cold Leg: 578 ft³

questions.question: What is the volume of the Core Region?
questions.expected_answer: The volume of the core region (fuel assembly region and reflector cooling channels) is 89 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Volumes
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Core Region (fuel assembly region and reflector cooling channels): 89 ft³

questions.question: What is the volume of the SG region?
questions.expected_answer: The volume of the SG Region is 621 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Volumes
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: SG Region: 621 ft³

questions.question: What is the total volume of the PZR region?
questions.expected_answer: The volume of the PZR region (main steam plenums, PZR, RPV top head) is 578 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Volumes
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: PZR Region (main steam plenums, PZR, RPV top head): 578 ft³

questions.question: What is the volume of the cylindrical section of the PZR region?
questions.expected_answer: The volume of the cylindrical section of the PZR region (main steam plenums and PZR) is 487 ft³.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Volumes
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: PZR Region, cylindrical (main steam plenums and PZR): 487 ft³

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym ECCS?
questions.expected_answer: ECCS stands for Emergency Core Cooling System.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS: emergency core cooling system

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym DHRS?
questions.expected_answer: DHRS is an abbreviation for Decay Heat Removal System.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS: decay heat removal system

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation CNV?
questions.expected_answer: CNV is an abbreviation for Containment Vessel.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CNV: containment vessel

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation RCS?
questions.expected_answer: RCS is an abbreviation for Reactor Coolant System.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RCS: reactor coolant system

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym LOCA?
questions.expected_answer: LOCA stands for Loss-Of-Coolant Accident.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: LOCA: loss-of-coolant accident

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation RPV?
questions.expected_answer: RPV is an abbreviation for Reactor Pressure Vessel.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RPV: reactor pressure vessel

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation PZR?
questions.expected_answer: PZR is an abbreviation for Pressurizer.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: PZR: pressurizer

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation SG?
questions.expected_answer: SG is an abbreviation for Steam Generator.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: SG: steam generator

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym RSV?
questions.expected_answer: RSV is an abbreviation for Reactor Safety Valve.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RSV: reactor safety valve

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym RVV?
questions.expected_answer: RVV is an abbreviation for Reactor Vent Valve.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RVV: reactor vent valve

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation RRV?
questions.expected_answer: RRV is an abbreviation for Reactor Recirculation Valve.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RRV: reactor recirculation valve

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym ASME?
questions.expected_answer: ASME stands for the American Society of Mechanical Engineers.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ASME: American Society of Mechanical Engineers

questions.question: What is the definition of the acronym RCPB?
questions.expected_answer: RCPB is an abbreviation for Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RCPB: reactor coolant pressure boundary

questions.question: What is the definition of the abbreviation NPM?
questions.expected_answer: NPM stands for NuScale Power Module.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: Acronyms and Abbreviations
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NPM: NuScale Power Module

questions.question: Explain the main components of NPM.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.1-1 shows the main components of the NPM, with the RPV located inside the CNV, and the core, steam generator, and pressurizer integrated within the RPV.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.1-1: NuScale Power Module Major Components
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NPM major components showing CNV, RPV, core, steam generators, and pressurizer arrangement

questions.question: Explain the main flow paths of the RCS simplified diagram.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.1-2 is a simplified diagram of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), illustrating the natural circulation path where the heated coolant rises through the riser, passes through the steam generator (SG), cools down, and then descends through the downcomer to return to the core.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.1-2: Reactor Coolant System Simplified Diagram
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RCS flow path showing natural circulation from core through riser, SG, and downcomer

questions.question: Describe the structure of the helical tube bundle in the steam generator.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-1 shows the SG helical tube bundle, with two independent helical coil tube bundles interconnected within the RPV.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-1: Steam Generator Helical Tube Bundle
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Helical coil tube bundle configuration of steam generators

questions.question: Explain the composition of the DHRS simplified diagram.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-10 is a simplified diagram of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), illustrating the configuration in which the DHRS heat exchanger is submerged in a pool and removes decay heat through steam and feedwater piping connected to the steam generator (SG).

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-10: Decay Heat Removal System Simplified Diagram
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS configuration with heat exchangers submerged in reactor pool

questions.question: Describe the structure of the NuScale Power Module.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-6 is a cross-sectional view of the NPM, illustrating the arrangement of the RPV within the CNV, and the sequential placement of the core, riser, SG tube bundle, and pressurizer region inside the RPV.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-6: Cutaway View of NuScale Power Module
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Cutaway view showing internal arrangement of NPM components

questions.question: Describe the flow of coolant between the steam generator and the reactor.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-7 illustrates the flow between the steam generator (SG) and the reactor, showing that the primary coolant flows downward outside the SG tubes, while the secondary feedwater flows upward inside the tubes and undergoes a phase change to steam.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-7: Steam Generator and Reactor Flow
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Primary and secondary coolant flow paths through steam generators

questions.question: Describe the configuration of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS).
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-8 illustrates the configuration of the DHRS, where the DHRS heat exchangers connected to each SG are submerged in the reactor pool, passively removing decay heat.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-8: Decay Heat Removal System
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS heat exchangers submerged in reactor pool for passive cooling

questions.question: Describe the composition of the ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System).
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-9 illustrates the configuration of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), with three Residual Heat Removal (RHR) valves located at the top of the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and two Residual Heat Removal (RHR) valves positioned on the side of the RPV, facilitating the recirculation of water from the Containment (CNV) to the core during an emergency.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-9: Emergency Core Cooling System
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS valve arrangement with RVVs and RRVs for emergency cooling

questions.question: Explain the Conceptual Site Layout.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-1 is a conceptual site layout of the NuScale power plant, illustrating the arrangement of key buildings such as the Reactor Building, Control Building, and Turbine Building.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-1: Conceptual Site Layout
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Site layout showing major buildings including Reactor, Control, and Turbine Buildings

questions.question: Describe the multi-module NuScale power plant layout.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-4 illustrates the multi-module layout of the NuScale power plant, where up to 12 Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs) are positioned in a common pool, with each module connected to an individual turbine generator.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-4: Layout of a Multi-Module NuScale Power Plant
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Multi-module configuration with up to 12 NPMs in common pool

questions.question: Describe the cross-sectional view of the configuration consisting of 12 modules.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-5 is a cross-sectional illustration of a configuration consisting of 12 modules, depicting the arrangement of 12 NPMs in the common pool within the Reactor Building.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-5: Cutaway Illustration of 12 Module Configuration
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Cutaway showing 12 NPMs arranged in common pool within Reactor Building

questions.question: Describe the structure of the reactor vessel.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.3-1 illustrates the reactor vessel structure, which consists of the upper head, shell, and lower head. Within this structure, the core, reactor vessel internals (RVI), steam generator (SG), and pressurizer regions are arranged.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.3-1: Reactor Vessel
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Reactor vessel structure with upper head, shell, and lower head

questions.question: Explain the simplified diagram of the Reactor Safety Valve (RSV).
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.2-1 is a simplified diagram of the RSV, illustrating a two-stage structure consisting of a pilot valve and a main valve.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.2-1: Reactor Safety Valve Simplified Diagram
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RSV two-stage structure with pilot and main valve

questions.question: Describe the configuration of the steam generators within the upper reactor pressure vessel (RPV).
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-2 illustrates the configuration of the steam generators (SG) within the upper region of the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), depicting an arrangement where two helical tube bundles are interconnected in the upper area of the RPV.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-2: Configuration of Steam Generators in Upper Reactor Pressure Vessel
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Two helical tube bundles configuration in upper RPV section

questions.question: Describe the structure of the main steam and feedwater plenum.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-3 illustrates the main steam and feedwater plenum structure, featuring a steam plenum at the top and a feedwater plenum at the bottom, which connects the SG tubes to the external piping.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-3: Main Steam and Feedwater Plena
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Steam plenums at top and feedwater plenums at bottom connecting SG tubes

questions.question: Describe the pressurizer region of the reactor vessel.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.4-17 illustrates the pressurizer region of the RPV, depicting the structure where the pressurizer heater and spray nozzles are located above the pressurizer baffle plate.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-17: Pressurizer Region of Reactor Vessel
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Pressurizer region showing heaters and spray nozzles above baffle plate

questions.question: Explain the schematic diagram of the secondary side equipment related to a single NPM (Nuclear Power Module).
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-3 is a schematic diagram of a single Nuclear Power Module (NPM) and its secondary equipment, illustrating the thermodynamic cycle in which steam from the NPM flows to the turbine and returns to the feedwater after passing through the condenser.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-3: Schematic of a Single NuScale Power Module and Associated Secondary Equipment
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Thermodynamic cycle showing steam from NPM to turbine, condenser, and back as feedwater

questions.question: Describe the location of the surveillance capsule.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.3-2 shows the location of the monitoring capsules, where several monitoring capsules are positioned around the core region inside the RPV to monitor material degradation due to neutron irradiation.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.3-2: Location of Surveillance Capsules
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Surveillance capsules positioned around core region for material monitoring

questions.question: Describe the normal heating and critical limit pressure-temperature curve.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 5.3-3 illustrates the normal heating and critical limit P-T curves, depicting the allowable pressure-temperature region during operation and the limiting lines to prevent brittle fracture.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.3-3: Pressure-Temperature Limits for Normal Heatup and Criticality Limit
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: P-T curve showing allowable operating region and brittle fracture prevention limits

questions.question: Explain the functional boundaries of NuScale.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-2 illustrates the functional boundaries of NuScale, depicting the interfaces between the Nuclear Island, the Turbine Island, and the Balance of Plant (BOP) areas, as well as the functional scope of each area.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-2: NuScale Functional Boundaries
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Functional boundaries showing Nuclear Island, Turbine Island, and BOP interfaces

questions.question: What are the main components to which the RCS design pressure and temperature are applied?
questions.expected_answer: The design pressure of the RCS is 2100 psia and the design temperature is 650°F, which apply to the internal components of the NPM as indicated in Figure 5.1-1, including the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), pressurizer, and the primary side of the steam generator.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Design pressure: 2100 psia, Design temperature: 650°F

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.1-1: NuScale Power Module Major Components
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NPM showing RPV, SG, PZR and other primary components

questions.question: Explain the principle of natural circulation cooling in the NuScale design.
questions.expected_answer: NuScale cools the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) through natural circulation. As shown in Figure 5.1-2, the coolant heated in the core rises and passes through the steam generator, releasing heat before descending, all occurring without the use of pumps.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Simplified, passive design with natural circulation for cooling

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.1-2: Simplified Reactor Coolant System Diagram
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RCS diagram showing natural circulation flow path

questions.question: Describe the material properties of the RPV (Reactor Pressure Vessel).
questions.expected_answer: The RPV is constructed from SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 low-alloy steel, and its interior is clad with austenitic stainless steel (SA-182 Grade F304) to ensure corrosion resistance.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.2-1: Reactor Pressure Vessel Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Material: SA-508 Gr 3 Cl 1 with SS cladding
RPV fabricated from low alloy steel with austenitic stainless steel cladding

questions.question: Describe the heat transfer area and structural characteristics of the steam generator.
questions.expected_answer: The steam generator (SG) consists of two independent helical coil tube bundles, which are positioned in the upper reactor pressure vessel (RPV) as shown in Figure 5.4-1. According to Table 5.4-1, the total heat transfer area and the number of tubes are specified.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-1: Steam Generator Tube Bundle Assembly
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Helical coil tube bundle configuration

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.4-1: Steam Generator Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Heat transfer area and tube specifications

questions.question: Explain the operating principle of the DHRS (Diverse Heat Removal System).
questions.expected_answer: The DHRS is a passive residual heat removal system that removes heat through natural circulation between the secondary side of the steam generator and the cooling water pool, as illustrated in Figure 5.4-10. It operates by opening valves without the need for external power.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS removes decay heat through natural circulation without AC power

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-10: Decay Heat Removal System Schematic
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: DHRS schematic showing passive heat transfer to pool

questions.question: Explain what ECCS is and how it operates.
questions.expected_answer: The ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) is a passive safety system that cools the core by opening the RVV and RRV valves in the event of an accident. The abbreviations are defined in Table 1.1-1.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: List of Acronyms
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS - Emergency Core Cooling System
Passive safety systems including ECCS with reactor vent and recirculation valves

questions.question: Describe the characteristics of the Operating Floor.
questions.expected_answer: As shown in Figure 1.2-4, the operating level of the Reactor Building features a crane and fuel handling equipment situated above the bio-shield structure, along with designated spaces for maintenance and water supply operations.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-4: Reactor Building Operating Floor
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Operating floor layout showing crane and fuel handling areas
Operating floor provides access for refueling and maintenance activities

questions.question: Analyze the thermal-hydraulic characteristics of the RCS (Reactor Coolant System).
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.1-1, the normal operating conditions for the RCS are a pressure of 1850 psia and a core exit temperature of approximately 590°F. The core thermal power of 160 MWt is removed through natural circulation flow and temperature difference.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Operating pressure, temperature, and flow data
RCS operates at natural circulation conditions to remove 160 MWt

questions.question: Explain the location and function of the pressurizer.
questions.expected_answer: The pressurizer is integrated into the upper part of the RPV and is located on top of the PZR baffle plate, as shown in Figure 5.4-17. It controls the RCS pressure through heaters and spray.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-17: Pressurizer Region of Reactor Vessel
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Pressurizer location above baffle plate in upper RPV
Integrated pressurizer in upper RPV controls RCS pressure via heaters and spray

questions.question: Explain the flexibility of the NuScale site layout and the number of modules.
questions.expected_answer: As shown in the site layout diagram in Figure 1.2-1, the NuScale power plant can be flexibly configured with between 1 and 12 modules, sharing a common Reactor Building and control room.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-1: Twelve-Module NuScale Power Plant Site Layout
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Site layout showing arrangement of 12 NPMs
Plant can operate from 1 to 12 NPMs in a single Reactor Building

questions.question: Explain the management of radiation exposure for RPV materials.
questions.expected_answer: To monitor the embrittlement of RPV materials due to neutron irradiation, surveillance capsules are installed around the reactor core as shown in Figure 5.3-2, from which specimens are periodically withdrawn and tested.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.3-2: Location of Surveillance Capsules
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Surveillance capsule locations around core
Material surveillance program monitors RPV embrittlement from neutron irradiation

questions.question: Explain the inventory of coolant in the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and the management of its chemistry.
questions.expected_answer: The RCS coolant is a boric acid solution, and the total inventory is specified in Table 5.1-1. Corrosion is minimized and reactivity is controlled through chemical control.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.1-1: Reactor Coolant System Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RCS coolant inventory specifications
RCS water chemistry control for corrosion minimization and reactivity control

questions.question: Describe the physical dimensions of the RPV.
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.2-1, the inner diameter, thickness, and height of the RPV are specified, and the structural arrangement of components such as the upper/lower heads, flanges, and nozzles can be observed in Figure 5.2-1.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.2-1: Reactor Pressure Vessel Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RPV dimensional specifications

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.2-1: Reactor Pressure Vessel General Arrangement
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: RPV structural arrangement showing heads, flanges, nozzles

questions.question: Explain the material of the steam generator tubes and their heat exchange performance.
questions.expected_answer: The SG tubes are made of Inconel 690 alloy, which exhibits excellent corrosion resistance. They achieve the required heat exchange performance under the thermal transfer area and design conditions specified in Table 5.4-1.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: SG tubes fabricated from Inconel 690 for corrosion resistance

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.4-1: Steam Generator Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: SG heat transfer area and thermal performance specifications

questions.question: Describe the design pressure and structure of the CNV.
questions.expected_answer: Table 5.2-2 specifies the design pressure and temperature of the CNV, and Figure 5.2-5 illustrates the arrangement of the upper and lower heads and the penetrations of the cylindrical CNV.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.2-2: Containment Vessel Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CNV design pressure and temperature specifications

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.2-5: Containment Vessel General Arrangement
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CNV structural arrangement with heads and penetrations

questions.question: Explain NuScale's safety design philosophy.
questions.expected_answer: NuScale adopts a passive safety design philosophy that performs safety functions without the need for external power or active pumps. Figure 1.2-5 illustrates the schematic configuration of passive systems such as the DHRS and ECCS.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Passive safety design requiring no AC/DC power or active pumps

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-5: Passive Safety Systems Overview
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Schematic showing DHRS, ECCS and other passive systems

questions.question: Explain the operational limitations of the P-T limit curve during normal operation.
questions.expected_answer: The P-T limit curve in Figure 5.3-3 defines the allowable operating range during normal startup/cooling. A pressure upper limit is specified according to temperature to prevent brittle fracture.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.3-3: Pressure-Temperature Limits for Normal Heatup
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: P-T curve defining allowable operating region
P-T limits established to prevent brittle fracture during heatup/cooldown

questions.question: Explain the role of the CVCS (Chemical and Volume Control System).
questions.expected_answer: The CVCS (Chemical and Volume Control System) is defined in Table 1.1-1 and performs functions such as controlling the boric acid concentration in the RCS, level control, and purification.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: List of Acronyms
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CVCS - Chemical and Volume Control System
CVCS controls boron concentration, coolant level, and provides purification

questions.question: Explain the advantages of modular design in NPM.
questions.expected_answer: As shown in Figure 1.2-3, the NPM is designed as an independent module, allowing for factory fabrication and on-site assembly. This design enhances flexibility through individual module operation and maintenance, maximizing utilization.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-3: Single NPM and Secondary Equipment
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Single NPM module schematic showing independent operation capability
Modular design enables factory fabrication and independent module operation

questions.question: Explain the design data and operating conditions for the secondary side of the steam generator (SG).
questions.expected_answer: According to Table 5.4-2, the secondary side of the steam generator specifies parameters such as feedwater temperature, steam pressure/temperature, and steam flow rate. Superheated steam is supplied to the turbine for power generation.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.4-2: Steam Generator Secondary Side Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Secondary side operating conditions including steam parameters
SG produces superheated steam for turbine power generation

questions.question: Explain the operation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) during a Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA).
questions.expected_answer: In the event of a LOCA, the RVV and RRV of the ECCS will open, forming a coolant circulation path between the RPV and CNV as shown in Figure 5.4-12, thereby passively cooling the core.

ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS opens RVV and RRV to establish circulation between RPV and CNV during LOCA

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-12: ECCS Flow Path During LOCA
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: ECCS flow diagram showing valve positions and circulation

questions.question: Describe the configuration of the NuScale control room.
questions.expected_answer: As shown in Figure 1.2-6, the NuScale main control room monitors and controls a total of 12 modules. There are individual operator stations for each module as well as a common monitoring display.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-6: Main Control Room Layout
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: MCR layout showing operator stations for 12 modules
Common MCR monitors and controls all 12 NPMs with individual operator stations

questions.question: Verify the abbreviations related to the NRC and explain NuScale's NRC licensing approach through the text.
questions.expected_answer: The abbreviation NRC (Nuclear Regulatory Commission) is defined in Table 1.1-1. NuScale is pursuing Design Certification and Combined License under NRC 10 CFR 52.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: List of Acronyms
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: NRC - Nuclear Regulatory Commission
NuScale pursues Design Certification under 10 CFR 52

questions.question: Describe the support structure of the steam generator tubes.
questions.expected_answer: In Figure 5.4-4, the SG tube support structure is shown, where the helical tube bundles are protected from flow-induced vibrations by multiple tube support plates.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.4-4: Steam Generator Tube Support Structure
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Tube support plates configuration for helical bundle
Tube support plates protect helical tubes from flow-induced vibration

questions.question: Explain the role of the Ultimate Heat Sink through the text and the table in Chapter 1.
questions.expected_answer: The Ultimate Heat Sink (UHS) serves as the final heat removal source in the event of an accident, with the reactor pool water fulfilling the UHS function. It is capable of passive cooling for more than 72 hours.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 1.1-1: List of Acronyms
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: UHS - Ultimate Heat Sink
Reactor pool serves as UHS providing 72+ hours of passive cooling

questions.question: Explain the purpose of maintaining CNV vacuum.
questions.expected_answer: The CNV maintains a vacuum (sub-atmospheric) condition during normal operation. The normal operating pressure conditions are specified in Table 5.2-2, which serves the purpose of providing insulation effects and ensuring pressure margins in the event of an accident.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.2-2: Containment Vessel Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: CNV normal operating pressure (sub-atmospheric)
CNV vacuum provides insulation and pressure margin during accidents

questions.question: Explain the fuel handling process.
questions.expected_answer: Fuel replacement is carried out at the operating layer shown in Figure 1.2-4. The Nuclear Power Module (NPM) is withdrawn from the pool, the flange is opened, and after replacing the fuel assembly with a crane, it is reassembled.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-4: Reactor Building Operating Floor
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Refueling operations area on operating floor
Refueling performed by removing NPM from pool and opening flange

questions.question: Explain the pressure control methods for the pressurizer heater and spray.
questions.expected_answer: The pressurizer heats the coolant to increase pressure using the heater capacity specified in Table 5.4-3, and lowers the pressure by condensing steam through spraying. This process maintains the RCS pressure within the normal range.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Table 5.4-3: Pressurizer Design Data
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Pressurizer heater capacity and spray flow specifications
Heaters increase pressure by heating coolant; spray condenses steam to reduce pressure

questions.question: Describe the specimen testing procedures for the materials surveillance program.
questions.expected_answer: Specimens are periodically extracted from the surveillance capsule located at Figure 5.3-2 to conduct impact tests (Charpy), tensile tests, and other evaluations. As a result, the changes in RTNDT are assessed to update the P-T limits.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 5.3-2: Location of Surveillance Capsules
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Capsule locations for periodic specimen withdrawal
Periodic Charpy and tensile testing to evaluate RTNDT shift and update P-T limits

questions.question: Explain the multi-module operation strategy of NuScale.
questions.expected_answer: Figure 1.2-1 shows the arrangement of 12 modules. The independent operation of each module allows for the operation of the remaining modules during maintenance of one module, thereby maximizing the plant's utilization rate.

ground_truth_evidence.caption: Figure 1.2-1: Twelve-Module NuScale Power Plant Site Layout
ground_truth_evidence.relevant_text: Layout showing 12 independent NPMs
Independent module operation allows maintenance without full plant shutdown
